data.title: These are the impacts of the markets ‘unwinding’ their 2025 ‘U.S. risk premium’ – GS
data.description:
The U.S. market (SP500), (COMP:IND), (DJI), (US10Y), (US2Y) has largely unwound its earlier concerns about structural risks that drove unusual trading patterns in early 2025, according to Vickie Chang, macro strategist in Goldman Sachs Research.

“The market looks to have sharply increased its estimation of the risk premium in U.S. assets in the wake of the reciprocal tariff announcement at the beginning of April, but has since largely unwound that move,” she said, noting that since late July, the pattern has shifted to “continued compression of that increased U.S. risk premium.”

 

 

 

Earlier this year, markets exhibited an unusual pattern where the U.S. dollar (DXY) weakened alongside U.S. equity (SP500), (COMP:IND), (DJI) and bond (US10Y), (US5Y), (US2Y) weakness, differing from standard monetary policy tightening shocks.

This correlation pattern is “consistent with what would be expected if the market demanded an increased risk premium for U.S. assets,” the strategist explained.

Several specific risks dominated market concerns in April and May, including tariff risks, fiscal concerns, and worries about Fed independence prompted by speculation about potential leadership changes. “In each case, the deeper tail risks have receded relative to that earlier period,” Chang said, while warning that “with the market having largely reversed its earlier fears, it would now be more vulnerable to even more modest challenges on these structural risks.”

These concerns could resurface, particularly around the expected Fed chair announcement in December or January. “Worries that the Fed might be easing too aggressively into a more robust growth or inflation backdrop could also fuel fears about these risks,” she said.

Similarly, “focus on fiscal risks could resurface too if tariff policies are constrained by the Supreme Court or fresh tax rebates are more seriously considered.”

If U.S. structural risks do reemerge, the market reaction could include “lower front-end yields and higher back-end yields, and so a steeper curve; a weaker U.S. dollar (DXY) particularly against reserve and safe-haven currencies; and higher gold (XAUUSD:CUR) prices,” Chang concluded.

However, she noted an alternative scenario: “If the Fed chair appointment reassures investors, this could lead the market to reduce its weight on these risks further,” potentially putting “upward pressure on U.S. front-end yields and potentially some support for the USD.”

data.title: Ford sees no impact to EBITDA, FCF from recent Novelis fire
data.description:
The day after a third fire at the Novelis aluminum plant in Oswego County, New York, Ford Motor (F) assured investors that the fire has not impacted its profit and free cash flow outlook for full year 2025.

“As of this morning, the cold mill and heat treatment operations at the Novelis Oswego plant are back up and running,” Ford (F) and Novelis said in joint statement filed with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission.

To mitigate any impact from the fire, Novelis will “continue to leverage alternate sources, including its global network of plants and industry peers,” the statement continues.

Accordingly, Ford (F) reaffirmed its FY25 adjusted EBITDA guidance of $6B to $6.5B and adjusted FCF of $2B to $3B.

After the September fire  at the Novelis plant, Ford (F) warned that the disruption to aluminum supplied to the automaker would cost the company as much as $1 billion.

The New York Novelis plant supplies almost two-thirds of the aluminum used in the manufacture of Ford’s F-150 lineup and up to 40% of the aluminum used in the auto industry.

data.title: Elanco wins Overweight rating, Zoetis Sector Weight as KeyBanc reviews animal health space
data.description:
Shares of Elanco Animal Health (ELAN) and Zoetis (ZTS) traded higher on Friday after KeyBanc Capital Markets initiated the rival veterinary product makers with Overweight and Sector Weight ratings, respectively.

Analyst Steve Dechert projects the global animal health market to record mid-single-digit growth from 2025 to 2033, driven by high-single-digit growth and 2%-4% growth in the Companion Animal and Farm Animal segments, respectively.

The analyst expects ELAN to improve margins thanks to a higher companion animal revenue mix and its Ascend cost savings program and argues that its leverage, following a sizable decline from ~5.5x in 2023 to 4x currently, will further improve due to higher profitability and cash flows.

Issuing a $27 per share target on ELAN, Dechert added that the company’s anti-inflammatory agent Zenrelia and other recent product launches could help Elanco (ELAN) generate 6%–8% annual growth in 2025–2028 compared to low-single-digit growth in 2022–2024.

While recognizing Zoetis’ (ZTS) strong financial position, above-average growth rate, and other positives, Dechert noted that the company continues to face pressure from its osteoarthritis pain drugs, Librela and Solensia.

“While we acknowledge ZTS's strong history of launching blockbuster products, we expect headwinds from the products to persist in the near term,” he wrote, pointing to the social media criticism linked to potential side effects of Librela and Solensia.

Dechert said he issues a Sector Weight rating on the stock until there is an improvement in osteoarthritis pain drugs and the company introduces products with high revenue-generating potential.

data.title: Federal Reserve gives public an extra month to weigh in on stress test proposal
data.description:
The Federal Reserve extended by one month the public comment period on its proposal to enhance transparency and accountability in bank stress-test models, it said on Friday.

The deadline was moved to Feb. 21, 2026, from the original Jan. 22, 2026.

Comments on the proposal regarding the 2026 stress test scenarios are still due Dec. 1, 2025, the central bank said.

The proposal is being led by Michelle Bowman, who has pushed for greater transparency in the stress-testing framework. Her approach stands in sharp contrast to former Michael Barr, the former Fed vice chair for supervision, who has warned that too much disclosure could weaken supervisory credibility. 

In September, Barr said he favors decoupling the U.S. central bank's stress testing framework from binding capital requirements to "maximize the informative value of the tests and their results and untangle them from the larger and complex task of setting capital requirement."

data.title: Market Voices: BLS cancels Oct. CPI, Fed rates, Nvidia CEO on China
data.description:
Seeking Alpha's roundup of statements, announcements, and remarks that could impact markets, sectors, and individual stocks. 

  • The Bureau of Labor Statistics said it won't be releasing the Consumer Price Index for October because it was unable to collect pertinent data during the government shutdown. 

The BLS said it plans to release the CPI for November on Dec. 18, but the news release and database update will not include certain month-over-month percentage changes. 

"With the November release, BLS will also finalize fourth quarter 2024 Chained CPI-U data series values and update 2025 first quarter through third quarter values. BLS has not yet determined a publication date for special research series, such as the CPI for new tenant rent and CPI for all tenant regressed rent," the BLS said in a post on its website. 

  • New York Federal Reserve President John Williams said he sees the Fed possibly lowering rates in the near term. 

“I view monetary policy as being modestly restrictive, although somewhat less so than before our recent actions,” Williams said in a speech on Friday, according to CNBC. “Therefore, I still see room for a further adjustment in the near term to the target range for the federal funds rate to move the stance of policy closer to the range of neutral, thereby maintaining the balance between the achievement of our two goals.”

  • Nvidia (NVDA) CEO Jensen Huang said that America needs access to Chinese markets to maintain its position as a world leader in AI.

"It is clear that we really need America to go back into the Chinese market to be able to compete there," Huang told Fox Business in an interview late Thursday. "It's good for the American people. It's good for the American tech stack. It's also good that [we're] able to compete in China so that we could also win around the world."

Huang noted that sales of Nvidia chips to China will be nonexistent for the next two quarters due to U.S. export restrictions. 

"I'm forecasting China's sales to be zero. It's zero for the next quarter, zero for the quarter after that," Huang said.

Huang added that the market opportunity for AI chips in China is around $50 billion and could expand to as much as $200 billion by 2030, according to Fox Business.

data.title: White House at odds with FDA Commissioner Makary top aide - report
data.description:
• FDA Commissioner Marty Makary is said to be pushing back at an effort from the White House to remove one of his top aides.
  • The White House is apparently unhappy that Associate Director of Policy and Research Strategy Sanjula Jain-Nagpal had told FDA stakeholders she is also Makary's deputy chief of staff, a role that the Trump administration has yet to approve, POLITICO reported.
  • Makary and Jain-Nagpal are known to be close as they both have connections to Johns Hopkins University.
  • The latest dustup involving the commissioner comes after a report earlier this month that HHS Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. considered limiting Makary's role and having someone else manage day-to-day FDA operations due to disagreements over his management style.
  • Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Airbnb chief technology officer leaving company
data.description:
• Airbnb’s (ABNB) chief technology officer Aristotle Balogh submitted his resignation to the company on November 18, according to a filing with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission Friday.
  • Balogh will remain in his position until at least February 2026 to provide transition and “other services” in an advisory role.
  • A successor has not yet been named.
  • Balogh has with Airbnb since 2018.

data.title: Lam Research opens $65M office in Oregon in response to expanding semiconductor demand
data.description:
Lam Research (LRCX), a semiconductor equipment manufacturer, opened the doors to its new $65M, four-story office building in Tualatin, Ore., on Friday.

The company highlighted the need for a new facility due to the demand surge in the semiconductor sector stemming from the rise of artificial intelligence and the data center boom.

"The addition of Building G in Tualatin expands one of Lam's essential global hubs for breakthrough semiconductor manufacturing equipment and process development, providing critical capacity for our Oregon workforce and accelerating our capabilities to drive innovations that underpin American leadership in an essential global industry," said Sesha Varadarajan, senior vice president, Global Products Group at Lam Research.  

Lam has historically had a presence in the Portland area, with locations in Tualatin, Hillsboro and Sherwood. The 52-acre Tualatin campus features two manufacturing sites and a research and development lab.

"Empowered by thousands of talented, hardworking Oregonians, Lam is on the cutting edge of delivering atomic-scale manufacturing advancements essential for the future of computing, robotics, AI, and more," said Oregon State Senate President Robert Wagner.

The new, 120,000-square-foot office space has capacity for 700 workspaces. Lam plans to expand its Tualatin lab as well. The company expects the semiconductor market to reach $1T in the next few years.

data.title: Wall Street rebounds after the prior day’s volatile downturn
data.description:
Wall Street rebounded higher on Friday, a day after the Nasdaq Composite saw a dramatic session due to a tech-led sell-off.

The tech focused Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was last up by 0.6%. At the same time, the benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) had inched up by 0.8% and the blue-chip Dow (DJI) advanced by 1.1%. 

From a sector-by-sector point of view, 10 of the 11 S&P segments are tracking higher in the green and are led by Health Care. At the other end of the spectrum, Info Tech has been the worst performer on the session so far. 

Treasury yields ticked lower as the longer-end U.S. 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) moved down by 2 basis points to 4.06% and the shorter-end U.S. 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) pushed down by 2 basis points to 3.51%. 

Market participants are also still digesting a turbulent session on Thursday that sent the Nasdaq Composite on a dramatic round trip—from up more than 2% in the morning to down over 2% at the close.

“While we expect volatility in 2026, we do expect a positive return with the S&P 500 reaching 7500 by the end of next year,” Mary Ann Bartels, chief investment strategist at Sanctuary Wealth said. 

“As we enter 2026, the equity market has had great performance since the lows of the bear market in 2022. We expect 2026 to be the reset year for the leadership of the market in the big Growth Technology stocks. We believe this reset will position stocks to continue their leadership into the end of the decade. Despite this anticipated correction in the large cap area of the market, we don’t see leadership emerging from small caps,” Mary Ann Bartels added. 

On the economic front, there are a few Federal Reserve speakers lined up, including Dallas Fed President Lorie Logan and Boston Fed President Susan Collins. 

The S&P Global U.S. Composite PMI rose slightly to 54.8 in November from 54.6 in the prior month. At the same time, Services PMI came in at 55 and manufacturing PMI came in at 51.9.

As for stocks that are on the move, shares of Intuit Inc (INTU) are higher by 5.6%, while shares of Oracle (ORCL) have slid lower by 6.2%.

data.title: Chip, AI stocks mixed, while broader market shows positive sentiment
data.description:
Chip stocks were mixed on Friday despite the larger market seeing some gains after yesterday's decline.

AI chipmakers, Nvidia's (NVDA) stock was largely flat but in the red, while Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) declined around 1%. Broadcom (AVGO) dipped about 1%, but Qualcomm (QCOM) seemed to buck the trend and rose nearly 2%.

The tech focused Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) climbed around 0.8%. At the same time, the benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) rose around 1%. The blue-chip Dow (DJI) had advanced by about 1.2%.

Shares of GlobalFoundries (GFS) soared nearly 5%, Texas Instruments (TXN) surged about 4%, and Lattice Semiconductor (LSCC) jumped around 3%. Analog Devices (ADI) climbed nearly 2%, while Micron Technology (MU), Marvell Technology (MRVL) and Intel (INTC) rose about 1% each. Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM) fell nearly 1%.

Software makers Microsoft (MSFT) and Palantir Technologies (PLTR) each dipped around 1%. However, Adobe (ADBE) rose about 4%.

Chip equipment makers: Applied Materials (AMAT) climbed about 2%, while Lam Research (LRCX) was also in the green. KLA (KLAC) and ASML (ASML) each fell nearly -2%.

On Thursday, a bipartisan group of U.S. lawmakers unveiled a bill in the House that would restrict the purchase of Chinese chipmaking equipment by CHIPS Act grant recipients for 10 years.
